layman_explanation: Spectral centroid indicates where most of the sound energy is concentrated in the frequency range. A higher value means the cough sounds sharper or brighter. A lower value means the cough sounds deeper or more muffled.
scientific_explanation: Spectral centroid represents the weighted mean frequency of the power spectrum. It is often interpreted as the 'center of mass' of the frequency distribution. Higher centroid values indicate greater high-frequency energy concentration, while lower centroid values indicate dominance of low-frequency components. It provides insight into spectral brightness and airflow turbulence characteristics.
physiological_context: Cough spectral brightness can be influenced by airway constriction, mucus presence, airflow turbulence, and glottal excitation patterns. Increased high-frequency components may result from turbulent airflow through narrowed airways, while lower centroid values may reflect smoother airflow or dominant low-frequency components.
dataset_context: Within SpectroCough, spectral centroid distributions vary across disease classes. Each class has a statistically modeled baseline mean and variance. Deviations are interpreted relative to the predicted class baseline to determine whether the cough exhibits unusually high or low spectral brightness.

deviation_logic.very_high: The cough contains significantly more high-frequency energy than typical samples of this class. This suggests increased spectral brightness or turbulence.
deviation_logic.moderately_high: The cough shows somewhat elevated high-frequency content relative to the class baseline.
deviation_logic.normal_range: The frequency center of mass falls within the statistically expected range for this class.
deviation_logic.moderately_low: The cough contains slightly less high-frequency energy than average for this class.
deviation_logic.very_low: The cough shows significantly reduced high-frequency content compared to the class baseline, indicating a more low-frequency dominated signal.

interpretation_guidance: Spectral centroid should be interpreted alongside spectral bandwidth and contrast. A high centroid combined with high ZCR may indicate strong turbulence. It contributes to the overall acoustic signature but does not independently determine classification.
counterfactual_template: To better match the target class profile, the spectral brightness (centroid) would need to {direction} toward that class's statistical mean.
visualization_note: In radar visualization, spectral centroid represents the brightness dimension of the cough signal.
tooltip: Frequency center of mass (brightness of cough).
